text.title: Agent Kill Switch Design Core Rules
text.summary: Four testable rules every application of Agent Kill Switch Design enforces. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Every Agent Kill Switch Design safety control has documented activation criteria, blast radius, and reversal path.

rationale.source: safety-critical systems / Leveson, Engineering a Safer World (2012)

rule.rationale: Controls without reversal create worse incidents than the original failure (data loss, perma-block).

rule.statement: Controls are drilled at least quarterly outside any real incident. Untested controls have 30%+ failure rate at first real use.
rule.rationale: Drills reveal credential expiry, IAM drift, runbook decay. Real incidents are the worst time to discover them.

rule.statement: Controls have audit logs that record who invoked, when, why, and outcome. Invocations without trace are non-events.
rule.rationale: Untraced controls become political ('was it kill-switch, or did it just break?'). Logs settle the question.

rule.statement: Multi-tenant controls have per-tenant scope by default — global activation requires explicit step and approval.
rule.rationale: Global kill-switches activated by mistake kill everyone. Per-tenant default contains the blast.

example.title: Kill switch with scope, criterion, reversal
example.code: Control: agent-kill-switch-tenant-X Activation: error_rate(tenant=X) >5% over 5min OR manual override Reversal: 1-click 're-enable' after eval rerun passes baseline Audit: log entry with invoker, reason, timestamp
example.note: Per-tenant scope contains the blast. Reversal is concrete.

example.title: Global manual kill with no reversal plan
example.code: Control: KILL ALL AGENTS Reversal: 'restart service'
example.note: Will be invoked by mistake. Reversal is hand-wavy.
